g.data-narration: Welcome to Deadlocks in Operating Systems. Today we'll explore deadlock conditions, prevention, avoidance, and recovery strategies.
g.text: Understanding Coffman Conditions, Banker's Algorithm & Recovery

g.data-narration: A deadlock occurs when processes wait indefinitely for resources held by each other, creating a circular waiting chain that halts the system.
g.text: A system state where two or more processes are permanently blocked, each waiting for resources
held by the others. No process can proceed without external intervention.

g.data-narration: Like four cars at an intersection, each waiting for the car on its right to move first - creating a traffic deadlock.
g.text: Each car waits for the car on its right

g.data-narration: Resource Allocation Graphs visually represent process-resource relationships using circles for processes and rectangles for resource types.
Four Coffman conditions must ALL hold simultaneously for a deadlock: Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, and Circular Wait.
g.text: All four conditions must hold simultaneously for deadlock to occur:

g.data-narration: Mutual Exclusion: Resources like printers cannot be shared. Hold and Wait: Processes keep resources while waiting for more.
g.text: At least one resource must be non-sharable.
Example: Printer, CD drive, database lock
Processes hold resources while waiting for others.

g.data-narration: No Preemption: Resources can only be released voluntarily. Circular Wait: A circular chain of processes waiting for each other's resources.
g.text: Only voluntarily released by holding process.
∃ set {P₁,P₂,...,Pₙ} where Pᵢ waits for Pᵢ₊₁'s resources
and Pₙ waits for P₁'s resources.

g.data-narration: Prevention designs systems to violate at least one Coffman condition. Each condition has a corresponding prevention technique.
g.text: Design system to violate at least one Coffman condition:
Engineering Tradeoff: Reduced throughput due to strict policies

g.data-narration: The Banker's Algorithm uses four key data structures: Available, Maximum, Allocation, and Need matrices to track system resources.
g.text: Four matrices track resource allocation state:

g.data-narration: The Safety Algorithm checks if the system can allocate resources without entering deadlock by finding a safe sequence of process execution.
When a process requests resources, the algorithm tentatively allocates, checks safety, then either commits or rolls back the request.
g.text: When process Pᵢ makes request Requestᵢ:

g.data-narration: Let's trace through a safety check: With Available=(3,3,2), process P3 with Need(0,1,1) can complete first, releasing its resources.
g.text: Given: Available = (3,3,2), check if safe sequence exists:

g.data-narration: Detection algorithms find deadlocks by checking system state. Single-instance resources use wait-for graphs; multiple instances use Banker's-like algorithm.
g.text: Use Wait-For Graph: Pᵢ → Pⱼ means Pᵢ waits for Pⱼ
Mark unmarked processes whose Request ≤ Work

g.data-narration: Recovery uses process termination or resource preemption. Victim selection considers priority, computation time, and resources held.
